BASCHERINI vs. USF, ET AL. [SFSC #CGC-18-567239]- MPA PETITION FOR ORDER TO
ARBITRATE; FOR DISMISSAL OF COMPLAINT OR, STAY OF ACTION PENDING ARBITRATION
$:\Client\101 USF\095 Bascherini\Pleadings\Petition to Compe! Arbitration\MPA.doc

 
1 L INTRODUCTION

2 Defendants University of San Francisco (“the University”) and Charles White (“Mr.

3 || White”) (collectively, “Defendants”) submit this Memorandum of Points and Authorities in

4 ||support of his petition, filed under the Federal Arbitration Act (“FAA”) and the California

5 || Arbitration Act (“CAA”), for an order compelling arbitration of the action filed against them by

6 || Plaintiff Robert Bascherini (“Plaintiff”).

7 In addition to an order compelling arbitration, Defendants seek an order under the FAA

8 || dismissing the action and, in the alternative, an order under the California Arbitration Act either

9 || dismissing or staying the action pending arbitration.

10 Il FACTS

11 The University of San Francisco (“the University”) is a fully-accredited institution of

12 ||higher education, and San Francisco’s oldest University, drawing thousands of students from

13. || states outside of California and foreign nations. Declaration of Diane Nelson (“Decl. Nelson”),

14 |) 42.

15 Plaintiff alleges that he became a University employee beginning “on or about March

16 ||26, 2009... until his May 24, 2017, termination.” Compl. p. 3:3-6.

17 Plaintiff alleges that Defendant Mr. White is “an individual who was at all times relevant

18 ||\the Senior Director of Recreational Sports at USF.” Compl. p. 2:8-10,. Plaintiff alleges that

19 ||“each of the Defendants herein was the agent, servant, licensee, or employee or alter ego, of

20 |leach of the remaining Defendants, and was at all times herein mentioned acting within the

21 ||course and scope of said relationship [, and that] each entity or employer . . . . allowed said

22 || wrongful conduct to occur and continue to occur... .” Compl. p. 2:15-23.

23 Plaintiff alleges that the University and Mr. White engaged in the actionable conduct

24 |\alleged in the Complaint. Compl. p. 2:6-14, This conduct includes:

25 Plaintiff alleges that Defendants’ treated Plaintiff, a Mexican-American male, in a

26 ||disparate manner from and less favorably in the terms and conditions of his employment than

27 |\his similarly situated non Mexican-American co-workers. Compl. p. 3:10-12. Defendants

28 |lallegedly “denied [Plaintiff] credit for his workplace accomplishments and in fact subjected

1 || [him] to manufactured allegations of inept performance and inappropriate conduct.” Compl. p.
2 |/3:13-15.
3 Plaintiff alleges that he “lodged a formal complaint with [Mr. White];” “[Mr.] White
4 |lignored Plaintiffs complaint;” and, “[t]hereafter Plaintiff directed his complaints to USF’s
5 || Human Resources Department.” Compl. p. 3:15-17. Plaintiff alleges the stated reason for the
6 || termination, performance issues, was pretext intended to mask “retaliation for his complaint . . .
7 ||. of racially motivated harassment.” Compl. p. 3:17-21. The University and its agent, Mr.
8 || White, alleged actions were in violation of the law for failing “to investigate [Plaintiff's
9 || workplace complaint],” and “terminat[ing] Plaintiff's employment on May 24, 2017.” Compl.
10 || p. 3:19-20.
11 Plaintiff alleges that he “properly brings forth this civil action to remedy the injuries he
12 ||has suffered as he has exhausted administrative remedies.” Compl. p. 4:1-2. Plaintiff's
13. || Complaint alleges five causes of action: 1) wrongful termination in violation of public policy, 2)
14 |lrace discrimination in violation of the Fair Employment and Housing Act (“FEHA”), 3)
15 ||Retaliation in violation of FEHA, 4) defamation, and 5) intentional infliction of emotional
16 || distress. Compl. p. 1.
17 Plaintiff and the University are parties to a written employment agreement
18 ||(“Agreement”) that provides, “[a]ny and all legal disputes initiated by either you or the
19 || University must be submitted only to binding neutral arbitration, before a mutually chosen
. 20 ||arbitrator, and not to a court, judge, or jury.” Decl. Nelson, { 10; Exhibit A.
21 Plaintiff executed Defendant USF’s two-page Agreement by signing it on December 20,
22 ||2012, a day after receiving it, and returning it to the University’s Human Resources Office.
23 || Decl. Nelson, ff 8-12; Exhibit A.
24 The letter instructed Plaintiff to “confirm your acceptance of this offer by signing,
25 ||dating, and returning this letter to the Human Resources Office.” Defendant signed and
26 || returned the two-page Agreement a day after receiving it. Defendant then worked for over four
27 || years under the terms of the Agreement. At no point was the Agreement ever modified. Decl.
28 || Nelson, ff 11, 14.
BASCHERINI vs. USF, ET AL. [SFSC #CGC-18-567239]- MPA PETITION FOR ORDER TO
ARBITRATE; FOR DISMISSAL OF COMPLAINT OR, STAY OF ACTION PENDING ARBITRATION
$AClient\101 USF\095 Bascherini\Pleadings\Petition to Compel Arbitration\MPA doc -2-

 
1 The Arbitration Provision states:
2 “Any and all legal disputes initiated by either you or the University must be
submitted only to binding neutral arbitration, before a mutually chosen arbitrator,
3 and not to a court, judge or jury. Both parties have the right to be represented by
counsel of their choice; each is entitled to discovery consistent with law; and to
4 obtain those remedies for claims and defenses as would be ordered by a court.
The University will pay all of the professional fees of the arbitrator unless you
5 object. The arbitrator will issue a written award that must be accompanied by a
written statement of decision. Legal disputes include those arising under this
6 contract or under federal, state and/or local statutes; the common law of
California; all contract and tort and other legal matters; but excluding
7 unemployment benefits, workers’ compensation benefits, the Labor Management
Relations Act of 1947, and EEOC, DFEH, and/or State Labor Commissioner
8 wage complaints, which complaints may be filed with the government agencies
9 notwithstanding this arbitration provision.” Exhibit A.
On June 21, 2018, Defendants demanded of Plaintiff, by letter to Plaintiff's counsel, that
10
Plaintiff agree to arbitrate the controversy and dismiss the action. Decl. Vartain, {{ 2-3.
11
Plaintiff's counsel has failed to respond to the demand. By failing to respond to the demand,
12
Plaintiff has refused to submit the matter to arbitration and dismiss the action.
13
The University is in commerce. Its annual revenues approximate $367 million. In 2017,
14
the University processed over $10 million in loans to students, funded through federal
15
government loan programs. The University enrolls and receives tuition payments from
16
thousands of out-of-state and international students. Decl. Nelson, { 3.
17
Of the nearly 1,500 students in the University’s 2017 freshman class, approximately
18
39% came from States other than California. The University also enrolls over 1,500
19
international students, representing 91 different countries. The University’s admission
20
department actively recruits prospective applicants from outside of California, employing
21
numerous out-of-state admissions representatives. Decl. Nelson, { 4.
22
The University offers online programs to students from around the world, with the
23
University faculty and staff teaching such courses and programs. Moreover, the University
24
offers study abroad and exchange programs worldwide, including programs in Senegal,
25
Morocco, Jordan, China, India, Japan, England, France, Germany, Argentina, Brazil, among
26
others. Decl. Nelson, 5.
27

$:A\Client\101 USF\095 Bascherini\Pleadings\Petition to Compel Arbitration MPA.doc ~3-

 
1 The University’s faculty are regularly recruited from all over the United States and the
2 || world. Many serve on out of state or international appointments or committees, frequently
3 ||traveling for those appointments, as well as for other University related purposes, such as
4 || academic conferences or seminars. Decl. Nelson, { 6.
5 Il, ARGUMENT AND ANALYSIS
6 A. Plaintiff and Defendant Executed An Arbitration Agreement That Is
4 Enforceable Under Both California Law and Federal Law.
8 As explained above, Plaintiff executed a valid employment agreement containing an
9 ||enforceable arbitration provision. The Agreement was clear, concise, and fair. The Agreement
10 || was a total of two pages. The Agreement stated,
11 “fa]ny and all legal disputes initiated by either you or the University must be
submitted only to binding neutral arbitration, before a mutually chosen arbitrator,
12 and not to a court, judge or jury .... The University will pay all of the
professional fees of the arbitrator unless you object .... This letter contains the
13 entire agreement between you and the University ... .”
14 Enforceable agreements include “agreements between employers and employees or
15 || between their respective representatives.” Cal. Code. Civ. Proc. § 1280(a). Arbitration
16 || provisions imposed as a condition of employment are enforceable absent a defense that exists to
17 || the formation of any contract. See Lagatree v. Luce (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 1105, 1137 (holding
18 || that arbitration provision is not an unconscionable provision as it does not limit the employee’s
19 || substantive remedies); see also 24 Hour Fitness, Inc. v. Superior Court (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th
20 || 1199 (predispute arbitration provision in an employment contract valid despite plaintiffs
21 || argument that arbitration agreement was unconscionable).
22 Defendants request the Court dismiss the action pursuant to the FAA. 9 U.S.C. § 1 ez.
23 ||seg. The FAA applies to any contract “involving commerce” among the several states or with
24 ||foreign nations. 9 U.S.C. § 2; Metaclad Corp. v. Ventana Environmental Organizational
25 || Partnership (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 1705, 1712. The phrase “involving commerce” in the
26 || FAA is the functional equivalent of the term “affecting commerce,” a term of art that suggests
27 |\the broadest permissible exercise of Congress’s commerce clause power. Allied-Bruce
28
BASCHERINI vs. USF, ET AL. [SFSC #CGC-18-567239]|- MPA PETITION FOR ORDER TO
ARBITRATE; FOR DISMISSAL OF COMPLAINT OR, STAY OF ACTION PENDING ARBITRATION
$:\Client\101 USF\095 Bascherini\Pleadings\Petition to Compel Arbitration\MPA.doc -4-

 
1 || Terminix Companies, Inc. v. Dobson (1995) 513 U.S. 265, 273-274; Shepard v. Mackay
2 || Enterprises, Inc., (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 1092, 1096.
3 Institutions of higher education are deemed to involve “interstate commerce.” Daghlian
4 || v. Devry University, Inc. (2007) U.S. Dist. LEXIS 98069, FN 21 (“[P]ostsecondary educational
5 || services in California constitutes ‘interstate commerce.’”). Such institutions involve the flow of
6 || people, money and information between states. Jd.
7 The University’s activities affect interstate commerce. In 2017, the University
8 || processed over $10 million in loans to students, funded through federal government loan
9 || programs. Of the nearly 1,500 students in the University’s 2017 freshman class, approximately
10 ||39% came from States other than California. The University also enrolls over 1,500
11 ||international students, representing 91 different countries. The University’s admission
12 ||department recruits prospective applicants from outside of California, employing admission
13 || representatives in other states.
14 The University offers online programs to students from around the world, with the
15 || University faculty and staff teaching such courses and programs. Moreover, the University
16 ||offers study abroad and exchange programs worldwide, including programs in Senegal,
17. || Morocco, Jordan, China, India, Japan, England, France, Germany, Argentina, Brazil, and other
18 || counties.
19 The FAA applies to the Agreement between the University and Plaintiff.
20 B. Mr. White Is Entitled to Enforce the Arbitration Agreement.
21 The Arbitration Agreement applies to the claims against Mr. White. “The agency
22 ||exception is another exception to the general rule that only a party to an arbitration agreement
23 ||may enforce it.” Garcia v. Pexco, LLC (2017) 11 Cal.App.Sth 782, 788, citing Thomas v.
24 || Westlake (2012) 204 Cal.App.4th 605, 613. “This exception applies . . . ‘when a plaintiff
25 |lalleges a defendant acted as an agent of a party to an arbitration agreement.’ ” Jd., citing
26 || Thomas, Cal.App.4th at 614. The rule of law is recognized by “both federal and California
27 || decisional authority.” Boucher v. Alliance Title Company, Inc. (2005)127 Cal.App.4th 262,
28 || 271.
BASCHERINI ys. USF, ET AL. [SFSC #CGC-18-567239]- MPA PETITION FOR ORDER TO
ARBITRATE; FOR DISMISSAL OF COMPLAINT OR, STAY OF ACTION PENDING ARBITRATION
S:\Client\101 USF\095 Bascherini\Pleadings\Petition to Compel Arbitration\MPA. doc -5-

 
1 Plaintiff alleges that Mr. White “is an individual who was at all times relevant the Senior
2 || Director of Recreational Sports at USF.” Compl. p. 2:8-10. Plaintiff further alleges, “at all
3 ||times herein mentioned, each of the Defendants herein was the agent, servant, licensee,
4 ||employee or alter ego, of each of the remaining Defendants, and was at all times herein
5 || mentioned acting within the course and scope of said relationship.” Compl. p. 3:15-18. Thus,
6 || Mr. Wright is entitled to enforce the arbitration agreement.
7 Cc. The Arbitration Provision Pertains to the Actions Alleged in the
g Complaint.
9 The Agreement broadly provides that “[a]ny and all legal disputes initiated by either you
10 || or the University must be submitted only to binding arbitration.”
11 Plaintiff's first cause of action alleges “Wrongful Termination In Violation Of Public
12 ||Policy.” Plaintiff alleges that Defendants’ conduct violated public policy pursuant to
13 ||“Constitution of the State of California, Article 1, section 8; Government Code section 12900,
14 |/et seq. (“FEHA”) ...; [or ] Constitution of the State of California, Article 1, section 1.” This
15 |{alleged claim arises under 1) the Agreement because it alleges Defendants wrongfully
16 ||terminated the Agreement, and 2) under state statute, the California Constitution and FEHA.
17. || The Arbitration Provision encompasses this claim pursuant to the terms of the Agreement.
18 Plaintiff's second and third causes of action are Race Discrimination and Retaliation in
19 || violation of FEHA. The second and third causes of action arise under state statute, and are
20 ||encompassed by the Arbitration Provision.
21 Plaintiff's fourth cause of action is defamation. In California, defamation may be
22 ||defined under the common law or California Civil Code section 44. Plaintiff does not refer to
23 |\the California Civil Code in the Complaint. Whether the alleged cause of action arises under
24 ||the common law or state statute is inconsequential, as the Arbitration Provision encompasses
25 || both.
26 Similarly, the fifth cause of action—intentional infliction of emotional distress—falls
27 || within the arbitration provision as a common law claim.
28 This broad arbitration provision encompasses the entirety of Plaintiff's Complaint.

1 IV. CONCLUSION
2 For the foregoing reasons, Defendants respectfully request an Order granting this
3 || petition to compel arbitration and dismissing the Complaint in its entirety or, in the alternative,
4 || staying this action pending arbitration.
